findings.description: Pagination offset is computed as page*page_size instead of (page-1)*page_size, so under the documented 1-based page contract the first page_size users can never be returned and every page is shifted by one.
User-supplied name_pattern is compiled and executed as an unbounded regex, enabling ReDoS via catastrophic backtracking (e.g. '(a+)+$') that can pin CPU for the whole service.
An invalid name_pattern makes re.compile raise re.error, which propagates unhandled to the caller instead of being rejected as invalid input.
page_size is only capped at MAX_PAGE_SIZE with no lower bound, so zero or negative values produce empty or nonsensical slice windows instead of a validation error.
page is never validated to be >= 1, and zero or negative pages yield negative slice offsets whose Python wrap-around semantics silently return wrong result windows.

search() fetches the entire (department-filtered) user table into memory and filters, sorts, and paginates in Python with no SQL ORDER BY/LIMIT/OFFSET, costing O(total users) memory and CPU on every request.
If display_name is NULL in the database, the sort key calls None.lower() (AttributeError) and line 60's matcher.search(None) raises TypeError, crashing the request.
deactivate omits 'AND deleted = 0', so deactivating an already-deleted user still yields rowcount 1 and returns True, making 'already deactivated' indistinguishable from a fresh deactivation.
by_email compares emails case-sensitively with no normalization, so lookups miss users whose stored email differs only in case.
departments() is typed list[str] but will include None if any user row has a NULL department.
re.IGNORECASE is hardcoded but undocumented in the docstring's 'name_pattern is a regex' contract, so callers needing case-sensitive matching silently get broader results than requested.
deactivate is a destructive mutation exposed on the search class with no authorization hook or audit logging, so any code path with search access can soft-delete arbitrary user ids.

A new sqlite3 connection is opened per operation with no timeout/busy handler, so concurrent writes (deactivate) can surface 'database is locked' errors and connection churn adds avoidable per-call overhead.
search returns every matching user's email with no field-level access control, a PII-exposure surface if the API is reachable by less-privileged callers.
Public methods by_email, departments, and deactivate lack docstrings while search has one.
Logging every search at INFO level is noisy for a hot read path; DEBUG would be more appropriate.
The file mixes typing.Optional with PEP 585 builtin generics (list[dict]); 'X | None' would be consistent on the Python versions this code targets.
SearchResult omits page_size/has_more metadata, forcing clients to recompute paging state themselves.
